title: Release builds trust mutable arm64e compiler prereleases
description: This commit changes the official XCFramework build path so release workflows build arm64e slices with a downloaded custom Rust stage1 toolchain. The stable and edge release workflows set ARM64E_STAGE1_FORCE_DOWNLOAD=1 and ARM64E_STAGE1_RELEASE_TAG=latest, so each release build dynamically selects the newest matching prerelease in cypherair/rust. The build script then downloads the compiler tarball and its SHA256 file from that same release, checks the tarball against the co-hosted checksum, extracts it, and executes the resulting rustc as part of cargo builds. There is no pin to an expected tag, digest, commit, or verified attestation before executing the compiler. A compromised or malicious cypherair/rust prerelease asset can therefore supply both a modified compiler and matching checksum, causing CI to execute attacker-controlled code and to publish backdoored arm64e slices in PgpMobile.xcframework. Because CypherAir protects OpenPGP private keys and plaintext, a backdoored FFI engine in the distributed framework could directly compromise confidentiality and integrity for users running arm64e device slices.
resolution_reason:
This finding should be closed as a false positive. It treats the referenced arm64e stage1 compiler prerelease source as an untrusted third-party dependency, but that trust boundary is incorrect for this project. The `cypherair/rust` stage1 prerelease channel is a first-party maintained toolchain input used intentionally by CypherAir release workflows, not an external dependency author or third-party package source.

Because the reported risk depends on treating that first-party toolchain channel as outside the project's trusted release infrastructure, the described issue does not represent a real reachable security vulnerability under the project's release trust model. Pinning an exact toolchain tag or digest could be considered reproducibility hardening, but it is not a required security fix for this finding.

title: Candidate gate ignores untracked build inputs
description: The commit adds a pre-archive validation intended to prove that an App Store candidate was built from the exact stable tag. However, the implementation runs `git status --untracked-files=no`, and the new tests assert that untracked files do not block candidate validation. This leaves build-affecting untracked or ignored inputs outside the provenance gate. In this project, the app target uses a `PBXFileSystemSynchronizedRootGroup` for `Sources`, so added local source files can be considered by Xcode, and the app links `PgpMobile.xcframework`, which is explicitly ignored by Git and is only checked for the existence of `Info.plist`. A malicious or stale untracked source file or replaced local XCFramework can therefore be included in an App Store candidate while validation still passes and `SourceComplianceInfo.json` reports the clean tagged HEAD commit. This undermines the source provenance guarantee and can allow a locally planted malicious build artifact to be shipped as if it matched the published stable source.
resolution_reason:
Closing as by-design / won't fix. The finding assumes that formal App Store candidate archives must prove byte-for-byte identity with the `PgpMobile.xcframework` published on the GitHub stable release page. That is not the project’s intended trust model.

For App Store candidates, the local maintainer build environment is trusted, and `PgpMobile.xcframework` is intentionally a locally generated artifact built through the documented `./build-xcframework.sh --release` path. The candidate gate is intended to prove the app source checkout, stable tag, release existence, and source-compliance metadata, not to require the local archive to consume the GitHub-published XCFramework binary asset.

Requiring every candidate archive to download and use or byte-compare the GitHub release XCFramework would add release fragility and operational cost without matching the project’s chosen release workflow. The residual risk is treated as release-operator discipline around refreshing local generated artifacts, not as a security vulnerability requiring a code change.

title: Reset leaves legacy contact files behind
description: Older CypherAir versions stored contact public certificates and contact metadata under the app Documents contacts directory and a contacts.quarantine directory. This commit removes the legacy contacts projection and migration path, but it also removes the reset service's knowledge of those legacy directories. As a result, users upgrading with existing legacy contact files can reset all local data and receive a successful reset even though those files remain on disk. The remaining files may expose the user's contact graph, names/emails from public certificate user IDs, and manual verification/trust metadata, and they violate the security expectation of a full local-data wipe.
resolution_reason: Closing as wont-fix/by-design. Current CypherAir Contacts state is the protected contacts domain under ProtectedData. Legacy flat files under Documents/contacts and Documents/contacts.quarantine were intentionally removed from the supported app-state model: the current app does not read, migrate, quarantine, trust, or reset-clean them. They cannot affect current contact resolution, certification state, recipient selection, or encryption behavior. This is an intentional legacy support cutoff rather than an active security issue.

title: PRs can warning-skip hosted Swift unit tests
description: The commit changes the PR and nightly Swift unit-test job from always running `xcodebuild test` after selecting Xcode to running it only when `scripts/ci_xcode_platform_preflight.sh macos-unit-test-preflight` emits `ready=true`. That preflight reads `MACOSX_DEPLOYMENT_TARGET` from the checked-out Xcode project and treats `host macOS < MACOSX_DEPLOYMENT_TARGET` as a skippable hosted-environment mismatch. In a pull_request workflow, the checked-out project is PR-controlled, so a contributor can raise the macOS deployment target above the hosted runner version and cause the job to exit successfully with `ready=false`. The workflow then skips artifact restore and `xcodebuild test`, emits only a warning, and the job can pass without running the Swift unit tests. This is a CI integrity issue rather than an app runtime vulnerability, but it weakens a security-relevant PR validation gate for Swift authentication, storage, and key-management code.
resolution_reason:
Close as wont-fix.

The finding correctly observes that hosted Swift unit tests can be warning-skipped on GitHub Actions. This is intentional for the current CI environment: GitHub-hosted Swift/macOS validation is not treated as the authoritative Swift test gate because the hosted runner/signing environment is not fully configured for this project.

We are not configuring the required signing credentials in GitHub Actions at this time due to credential-exposure concerns. Before publishing App Store candidate builds, full Swift/Xcode validation is run on trusted maintainer-owned Apple hardware instead.

Therefore this is an accepted CI environment limitation, not a planned fix in GitHub-hosted PR checks.

title: Mutable OpenSSL fork branch weakens supply-chain pinning
description: The OpenPGP engine uses Sequoia with the OpenSSL backend and the vendored openssl crate. This commit replaces the previous exact `rev` pin for the patched `openssl-src` fork with `branch = "carry/apple-arm64e-openssl-fork"`. That branch is mutable and sits in a downstream carry chain for OpenSSL target support. A normal build of this exact checkout should still use the commit recorded in Cargo.lock, but the manifest no longer cryptographically/immutably identifies the intended dependency. Any build path that regenerates or updates Cargo.lock, builds without the lockfile, or accepts a lockfile drift can silently pick up a changed branch head. Because this dependency provides the vendored OpenSSL code linked into the app's OpenPGP cryptography, a compromised or malicious update to that branch could affect confidentiality, integrity, signing, verification, or release artifacts.
resolution_reason:
Closing as false positive. The finding treats the `openssl-src` branch dependency as an untrusted third-party mutable dependency, but that is not the project’s trust boundary.

The `openssl-src` patch points to `cypherair/openssl-src-rs`, a first-party CypherAir downstream carry repository used intentionally for Apple arm64e OpenSSL target support. The branch is part of the project’s maintained release infrastructure, not an external dependency author source. Normal and release builds use the checked-in `Cargo.lock`, which records the resolved `openssl-src-rs` commit, and the XCFramework build path uses Cargo `--locked`.

Release automation also records the resolved `openssl-src-rs` commit and OpenSSL submodule commit in `PgpMobile.arm64e-build-manifest.json` and checks the carry chain freshness for release builds. Therefore the reported issue does not describe a real reachable vulnerability under the project’s first-party carry-chain trust model. Using an exact `rev` could be considered an alternative reproducibility policy, but the current branch-tracking model is intentional and not a security bug.

title: High-security app access downgraded to passcode fallback
description: Before this commit, AppContainer evaluated launch/resume authentication with config.authMode; users who selected AuthenticationMode.highSecurity therefore got biometric-only app access with no passcode fallback. This commit adds a separate AppSessionAuthenticationPolicy, but AppConfiguration initializes the new preference to userPresence unconditionally instead of deriving it from an existing highSecurity authMode or requiring an explicit migration choice. AppContainer now uses that new policy for app-session authentication, and ProtectedDataSessionCoordinator persists or migrates the app-data root secret using the same default policy. As a result, an existing user who had chosen High Security can be silently downgraded after upgrade: app launch/resume and newly persisted/migrated protected app-data root secrets use .deviceOwnerAuthentication / .userPresence, allowing device-passcode fallback where the previous high-security app gate required biometrics.
resolution_reason: This finding targets a one-time historical migration window from the old authMode preference to the newer separate App Access Protection policy. Current supported installs have already passed that migration path, and current High Security private-key protection remains enforced by the private-key-control protected domain. AuthenticationMode.highSecurity still gates private-key decrypt/sign/export with biometric-only access; App Access Protection is now an independent setting. We are closing this as not currently actionable for supported users. Legacy migration cleanup can be handled separately after the old-install support cutoff.

title: Authentication shield now uses translucent material
description: The authentication shield is meant to hide secure content while Face ID, Touch ID, passcode, or Secure Enclave authorization prompts are in progress. This commit replaces the previous opaque full-screen gradient with SwiftUI `.ultraThinMaterial`. Materials are translucent/blurred backdrops rather than opaque covers, so sensitive plaintext, contact metadata, filenames, key information, or decrypted-message UI can remain partially visible through the shield, especially as colors, layout, large text, or high-contrast content. Because the same shield is shown around privacy and operation prompts, this weakens the confidentiality boundary precisely when the app is asking the user to authenticate.
resolution_reason: False positive. The material blur is an intentional privacy-screen design choice, not an accidental weakening of the authentication boundary. I visually reviewed the current implementation and do not consider the current material shield to expose readable sensitive content or create a practical privacy/security leak. The finding treats “not fully opaque” as equivalent to a security failure, but that does not match the intended design or observed behavior.

title: Unbounded signature file import can exhaust app memory
description: The newly added ContactCertificateSignaturesScreenModel accepts .asc, .sig, plain text, and generic data files as signature input. When a file is selected, the default import action reads the entire URL into memory with Data(contentsOf:) and then attempts to create a UTF-8 String from the same bytes. There is no file-size preflight, streaming parser, or upper bound appropriate for OpenPGP detached certification signatures. A user who selects an attacker-supplied very large file through the new workflow can force excessive allocation and likely terminate the app due to memory pressure. This is an availability issue in a new attacker-controlled input path.
resolution_reason: Closing as false positive. This path requires the user to manually select an abnormally large local file in the certification-signature importer. Normal OpenPGP detached certification signatures are small, and the selected data is only used for local verification/import preview; it is not trusted, persisted, or able to affect cryptographic state unless it later verifies as a valid certification. A large local file can cause ordinary memory/availability pressure, but that is generic robustness hardening rather than a security issue for this offline, user-mediated workflow.

title: Vendored OpenSSL source redirected to external git fork
description: pgp-mobile builds OpenSSL in vendored mode, so openssl-src supplies the cryptographic C source and build logic used by Sequoia/OpenSSL. This commit adds a [patch.crates-io] override that redirects openssl-src from the checksummed crates.io package to https://github.com/cypherair/openssl-src-rs. Cargo.lock also loses the crates.io checksum for openssl-src and records a git source instead. Although the revision is pinned, the audited source is no longer contained in this repository nor protected by the crates.io checksum recorded in Cargo.lock. Release and XCFramework builds will compile and link cryptographic code obtained from that fork. If that fork revision is malicious or the dependency source is compromised before review, the attacker can alter OpenSSL behavior or execute build-time code, compromising generated app artifacts and cryptographic correctness.
resolution_reason:
Closing as false positive. The finding characterizes the `openssl-src` patch target as an external untrusted git fork, but that is not the project’s trust boundary.

The patch points to `cypherair/openssl-src-rs`, a first-party CypherAir downstream carry repository used intentionally to support Apple arm64e OpenSSL targets. That carry repository in turn tracks the CypherAir-maintained OpenSSL carry branch. This is part of the project’s maintained release infrastructure, not a third-party dependency source.

The resolved git commit is recorded in `pgp-mobile/Cargo.lock`, release builds use locked Cargo resolution, and release metadata records the resolved `openssl-src-rs` commit plus OpenSSL submodule commit in `PgpMobile.arm64e-build-manifest.json`. The project also performs release-time carry-chain freshness checks. Therefore this does not describe a real reachable vulnerability under the project’s first-party carry-chain trust model.

title: Unbounded detailed signature UI enables DoS
description: The new detailed signature section renders `verification.signatures` in full and first copies the whole enumerated collection into an `Array`. Because OpenPGP signed, detached-signature, and encrypted+signed inputs are attacker-controlled and the Rust collector records every observed signature result, a crafted input containing a very large number of signatures can force large Swift allocations and SwiftUI view construction. This can cause memory exhaustion, UI hangs, or app crashes when the user verifies or decrypts the file/message. The UI should cap the number of detailed signatures retained/displayed, avoid eager `Array(...)` copies, and show a truncation notice for excessive entries.
resolution_reason: False positive for CypherAir’s security review scope. The finding describes a local, user-mediated availability risk from explicitly verifying/decrypting maliciously large signature sets, but it does not expose plaintext/private keys, weaken signature correctness, bypass authentication, or cross the app sandbox. CypherAir has no network ingress or automatic remote processing path, so this is not treated as a user-data security issue.

title: Unbounded SKESK fallback enables password-decrypt DoS
description: Password-encrypted OpenPGP messages can contain multiple SKESK packets, and those packets are attacker-controlled input. This commit changes password decryption so that candidate errors and payload authentication/integrity failures are saved and the loop continues to later SKESKs. Because the code collects SKESKs without a count limit and calls decrypt_with_fixed_session_key for each derived candidate, a crafted message can include many syntactically valid SKESKs that produce candidate session keys but fail payload authentication. For SEIPDv1/MDC failures, decrypt_with_fixed_session_key reads the message into a plaintext buffer before detecting the integrity failure and zeroizing it, so the new fallback behavior can multiply CPU and memory work by the number of SKESKs. Previously, AEAD/MDC/unsupported-algorithm failures returned immediately. The fix should bound the number of SKESKs/candidate decrypt attempts, cap total work, or otherwise distinguish cheap reject paths from full-payload authentication retries.
resolution_reason:
Close as will-not-fix / out of target threat model.

CA21 describes a potential resource-exhaustion issue in the password/SKESK service path: a maliciously constructed password-encrypted OpenPGP message could include many SKESK candidates that cause repeated password-decrypt fallback attempts, including repeated full-payload decrypt attempts before the message is finally rejected.

We agree this could at most affect local availability if the path were exposed to untrusted user input: the app may hang, consume CPU/memory, or terminate. It does not create a confidentiality or integrity failure, does not expose private keys or plaintext, and does not bypass AEAD/MDC authentication. Partial plaintext from failed decrypt attempts is not returned to Swift callers and is zeroized on decrypt failure.

CypherAir’s current user-facing large-file and import paths already apply resource-conscious handling where those paths are part of the supported UI surface, such as streaming file operations and Argon2id memory checks for protected private-key import. The password/SKESK message feature, however, is currently service-only and is not part of the formally shipped UI surface. CypherAir does not plan to expose this password-message feature to users in the foreseeable future because the overall password-encryption product model does not meet our security expectations for a supported user-facing workflow.

For that reason, this issue is outside the current CypherAir threat model and will not be fixed at this time. If password/SKESK message support is ever promoted to a user-facing feature, this resource-budgeting concern should be re-evaluated before launch.

title: Imported-key revocations stored without export authentication
description: Revocation certificates are security-sensitive because anyone who obtains one can publish it to invalidate the user's OpenPGP identity. This commit generates a key-level revocation signature during secret-key import and stores it inside PGPKeyIdentity metadata. KeyMetadataStore saves that metadata with accessControl: nil, so it is only protected by the generic WhenUnlocked keychain accessibility rather than the biometric/passcode-gated Secure Enclave wrapping used for private keys. exportRevocationCertificate also returns any non-empty stored revocation certificate directly, without unwrapping the private key or requiring per-operation authentication. As a result, imported keys that previously had no stored revocation material now gain a revocation certificate that a local attacker with access to the app's unlocked keychain/app state can export or read without satisfying the private-key authentication policy, enabling unauthorized revocation of the user's imported PGP identity.
resolution_reason: Closing as by-design / false positive (verified at current HEAD 40ea2fa). Exporting a stored revocation certificate without a per-operation private-key unwrap is intentional — a revocation certificate is public-distributable material the user exports specifically to hand out, so it is not gated behind the decrypt/sign Secure Enclave boundary. The finding's medium escalation rests on the stored revocation living in an accessControl: nil (WhenUnlockedThisDeviceOnly) Keychain row; that premise is stale at HEAD: production persists key metadata in the ProtectedData key-metadata domain (AES-GCM, openable only after the app-session ProtectedData unlock, FileProtectionType.complete), and the legacy nil-accessControl KeyMetadataStore is now only a migration source / UI-test default with no production caller. (Also, deleteKey itself requires no per-operation private-key unwrap, so the app-session unlock needed to reach the key list is the consistent boundary — a local-unlocked-app attacker already has a strictly more destructive capability.) Offline + local-only + user-mediated + public-distributable design → residual risk low; there is no missing gate to add.

title: Unbounded armored text imports can exhaust memory
description: Armored text import now reads user-selected files with Data(contentsOf:) and then creates a UTF-8 String copy while also retaining the original Data in ImportedTextInputState. In DecryptView's file-mode inspection, the intended 256 KiB pre-read limit is undermined because attributes[.size] is converted with NSNumber.intValue, a 32-bit conversion. Very large files can wrap negative or otherwise truncate, pass the `fileSizeValue <= maxInspectableFileSize` guard, and then be read entirely into memory. In text-mode imports for both decrypt and verify, no file-size check is performed before Data(contentsOf:) at all. A maliciously supplied large .asc/plaintext file, or a large encrypted file selected in file mode, can therefore trigger app memory exhaustion/crash.
resolution_reason:
Close as false positive.

The finding conflates text-import convenience paths with the app’s large-file workflows. Current Decrypt file mode uses path-based streaming: recipient matching reads file headers only, and Phase 2 file decrypt streams from the selected file into an app-owned temporary artifact. The small “open as text?” inspection path is capped at 256 KiB before reading file contents, and the alleged NSNumber.intValue truncation bypass does not reproduce on the current macOS/Swift SDK because intValue is a 64-bit Int.

The only remaining behavior is that explicit text-mode imports (“Import .asc File” in Decrypt text mode and “Import Signed File” in Verify cleartext mode) read the selected local file into Data and String. That path is intentionally for text input, not arbitrary large files. A user-selected oversized local text file could cause local memory pressure or process termination, but it does not bypass AEAD/MDC authentication, expose plaintext or private keys, weaken signature verification semantics, or affect the streaming file decrypt/verify paths.

This is therefore a local user-mediated robustness/UX concern, not a CypherAir security boundary issue.

title: macOS Argon2 guard treats total RAM as available
description: For passphrase-protected secret-key import, Swift first parses S2K parameters, runs Argon2idMemoryGuard.validate, and only then calls the Rust import path that decrypts the secret key. The commit adds a macOS SystemMemoryInfo branch that returns ProcessInfo.processInfo.physicalMemory. That value is total installed RAM, not available memory or process headroom. Because validate permits Argon2id memory costs up to 75% of the returned value, a malicious secret key can choose a memory parameter that is below 75% of total RAM but above what is actually available. On import, Sequoia's decrypt_secret then performs Argon2id with that memory cost, which can hang or crash the app and create severe system memory pressure. The attacker must convince the user to import a malicious passphrase-protected secret key, so this is an availability issue rather than a confidentiality or integrity bypass.
resolution_reason:
Closing as by-design / wont-fix.

The finding correctly observes that the macOS implementation of `SystemMemoryInfo` returns total physical memory rather than current process-available memory. That behavior is intentional. The Argon2id memory guard is documented and designed as an iOS Jetsam protection: on iOS-family platforms it uses `os_proc_available_memory()` to avoid system termination before expensive Argon2id key-import work begins. macOS does not expose the same Jetsam model, and the current macOS adaptation deliberately uses physical memory as the capacity boundary.

The reachable impact is limited to local, user-mediated availability during passphrase-protected private-key import. A pathological Argon2id S2K parameter may cause memory pressure, swap, or process termination on macOS, but it does not bypass authentication, expose private keys or plaintext, weaken AEAD/MDC hard-fail behavior, or affect the app’s zero-network security model.

Future macOS-specific responsiveness limits or conservative import caps can be considered as robustness/product hardening, but this is not a security boundary violation in the current design.

title: Enhanced Security entitlements renamed to ignored keys
description: Entitlement names are exact identifiers. This change does not merely change the value type from integer to string; it removes `com.apple.security.hardened-process.enhanced-security-version` and `com.apple.security.hardened-process.platform-restrictions` from `CypherAir.entitlements` and adds different keys ending in `-string`. The repository documentation states the unsuffixed keys are the required Xcode-written keys and that removing required entitlement keys disables the corresponding Enhanced Security protections. Since the app relies on Enhanced Security/MIE as defense-in-depth for native C/C++ code paths such as vendored OpenSSL and untrusted OpenPGP parsing, losing platform restrictions or version gating weakens runtime hardening on supported devices.
resolution_reason:
Close as false positive.

The project’s MIE / Enhanced Security configuration is correct. The committed entitlement keys, including the `*-string` hardened-process keys, are the current Xcode/Apple-documented Enhanced Security entitlements, and the target build settings enable `ENABLE_ENHANCED_SECURITY` / pointer authentication. This configuration is compatible with Apple documentation and App Store review expectations.

title: Hidden QR key can override pasted contact import
description: AddContactView now stores binary QR/file key material in a global importedKeyData state and addContact() always prefers that state over the visible armoredText. The importMode onChange handler clears importedKeyData when the user switches modes, but processQRPhoto() runs asynchronously and can set importedKeyData after the user has already switched to Paste mode. In Paste mode the Add button is controlled only by armoredText, so a user can paste a legitimate public key, see that pasted text, and press Add Contact while addContact() actually imports the hidden binary QR key that arrived late. If an attacker can get the user to briefly select an attacker-controlled QR image and then the user switches to paste a trusted key before processing completes, the app may silently trust/import the attacker's key instead of the visible key, causing future encryption to target the wrong certificate.
resolution_reason:
Closing as false positive after user review.

The reported path depends on an unrealistic and effectively unreachable timing sequence: the user selects a QR image, the QR scan remains pending long enough for the user to switch to Paste, and then the hidden QR result overrides the pasted contact import. In the actual user flow, selecting a QR image returns and decodes essentially immediately as the picker dismisses; there is no practical shipped path where the user can switch to Paste before the QR result is presented.

Also, the import confirmation UI shows the actual key being imported, so this does not demonstrate a silent hidden-key override. This is distinct from CA-23, which is the separate shared confirmation-coordinator replacement issue already recorded for later fix.

title: Predictable decrypt temp file enables plaintext and symlink attacks
description: The streaming `decrypt_file` implementation stages plaintext at `format!("{output_path}.tmp")`. That path is predictable, located next to the caller-selected output, and is opened with `File::create`, which follows symlinks and truncates existing files. During streaming decryption, plaintext is written to this temp path before the message has fully completed integrity/authentication processing. If the output directory is attacker-writable or shared, an attacker can pre-create `<output>.tmp` as a symlink to another file accessible to the app, causing truncation/overwrite, or monitor/read the predictable temp file while a tampered message is being processed. The cleanup helper also follows symlinks via `metadata`/`OpenOptions`, so its best-effort overwrite can zero the symlink target before removing only the link. A safer pattern would create an unpredictable temp file in a private app-owned directory or use `create_new`/no-follow semantics with restrictive permissions, then atomically move it only after successful completion.
resolution_reason:
Close as false positive.

The finding is based on a stale temp-path model. Current streaming decrypt does not use a predictable `<output>.tmp` path; it writes to `{output_path}.<64-bit-random-hex>.tmp`, deletes that staging file on failure/cancellation, and only renames it after successful decrypt authentication.

In the shipped app, `output_path` is not user-selected or external. Swift creates it under CypherAir’s app-owned temporary artifact directory: `tmp/decrypted/op-<UUID>/<sanitized filename>`, inside the app sandbox/container. External user-selected locations are only used later through explicit export after decrypt succeeds.

Therefore the reported predictable-temp/symlink attack path is not present in the current product.

title: Launch auth can show content before blur is enabled
description: The commit adds cold-launch authentication by keeping isBlurred initially false and then setting it to true inside .onAppear when requireAuthOnLaunch is enabled. Because ContentView is the base view and the overlay is conditional on isBlurred, the app can render its normal UI before the onAppear state update takes effect. This undermines the new launch-auth setting as a privacy control: someone with local access to an unlocked device can open the app and potentially see key/contact metadata, navigation state, or other sensitive UI briefly before the Face ID/Touch ID prompt and blur are applied. The lock state should be established before the first frame, for example by deriving the overlay condition directly from an initial locked state, initializing a lock flag from configuration outside onAppear, or rendering only the privacy/auth gate until the launch authentication succeeds.
resolution_reason: False positive. Even if a cold-launch first frame briefly renders before the privacy overlay appears, the current app architecture does not expose user-sensitive data at that point. Protected user data is stored behind ProtectedData and is only unlocked after app-session authentication succeeds. A pre-auth launch frame can at most show app chrome or locked/placeholder UI, not decrypted content, private-key material, contacts, or other protected user data.

title: Bad signatures no longer hard-fail verification
description: In `VerifyHelper::check()`, the bad-signature branch now sets `self.status = SignatureStatus::Bad` but returns `Ok(())`. This means `VerifierBuilder`/`DetachedVerifierBuilder` can complete successfully for cryptographically invalid signatures. The public APIs then return `Ok(VerifyResult { status: Bad, ... })`, and `verify_cleartext` may also return the signed content. Although callers that explicitly inspect `VerifyResult.status` can still detect the failure, the API is exposed as `Result<VerifyResult, PgpError>` / Swift `throws`, and the surrounding code still contains hard-fail error handling paths such as `map_err(|_| PgpError::BadSignature)`. This is a security-sensitive contract change: any existing or future caller that interprets a non-throwing verification call as a valid signature will accept tampered content.
resolution_reason: Closing as false positive. CypherAir intentionally treats standalone signature verification as a graded-result API: parse/setup failures throw, while cryptographic signature invalidity returns Bad/Invalid status for the UI to present. The shipped Verify UI inspects that status and displays invalid signatures as “Signature verification failed — content may have been modified”; it does not treat a non-throwing result as a valid signature. The hard-fail invariant applies to decrypt payload authentication failures such as AEAD/MDC failure, not to standalone signature verification results.

title: Malformed protected settings envelope can crash app
description: The commit adds ProtectedDomainEnvelope decoding for the protected clipboard setting. validateContract() only checks that schemaVersion and generationIdentifier are positive. open() then passes these decoded Int values into envelopeAAD(), where they are converted with UInt16(schemaVersion) and UInt32(generationIdentifier). In Swift these conversions trap when the Int is out of range. Because the envelope is read from persistent app data and open() is called inside a do/catch that expects recoverable errors, a malformed current/previous/pending protected-settings plist with a very large schemaVersion or generationIdentifier can terminate the process rather than being handled as ProtectedDataError.invalidEnvelope/recoveryNeeded. This is primarily a local availability issue because normal third-party apps should not be able to write the app sandbox, but it is still a robustness/security bug in the newly introduced protected-data parsing path.
resolution_reason: Closing as wont-fix. This requires local tampering or corruption of app-owned ProtectedData envelope files with out-of-range metadata values. Normal app flows do not produce such envelopes, and sandboxed third-party apps cannot write this storage. The impact is local availability/crash rather than disclosure, authentication bypass, or acceptance of forged protected data. Given the broad shared codec surface and low security value, we are not treating this as a security fix.

title: Unvalidated temp metadata can spoof build provenance
description: The new SourceComplianceOverrides.json path is produced by the App Store Candidate scheme pre-action, but the target build phase consumes it solely based on file existence. generate_source_compliance_info.py then uses values from that file as fallbacks for commitSHA, stableReleaseTag, and stableReleaseURL even when SOURCE_COMPLIANCE_REQUIRE_STABLE_RELEASE is NO, and without checking that the tag matches the current marketing version/build number or that commit_sha matches the current HEAD. As a result, a stale or locally planted file under TARGET_TEMP_DIR can cause a non-stable or mismatched build to embed misleading stable-release provenance in the app's SourceComplianceInfo.json. This does not directly change app code, leak secrets, bypass authentication, or publish an artifact by itself, but it weakens local release/compliance provenance integrity.
resolution_reason:
Closing as false positive. The reported path depends on stale or planted `SourceComplianceOverrides.json` metadata being consumed without the App Store candidate validation step. That is not the supported formal candidate flow.

The supported App Store candidate path uses the dedicated `CypherAir AppStore Candidate` scheme. Its pre-action runs `validate_app_store_candidate_release.py` and writes fresh `SourceComplianceOverrides.json` only after validating the main branch, clean tracked state, stable release existence, required arm64e manifest, and `HEAD` equality with the remote stable tag commit. The later source-compliance build phase then consumes that freshly generated metadata.

A stale temp metadata file would require bypassing or not using the dedicated candidate scheme, or otherwise tampering with local DerivedData/temp state. It does not affect the binary, cryptographic behavior, authentication, or release publication, and the documented release checklist also requires verifying the displayed Source & Compliance commit/tag/URL before upload. Therefore this does not describe a real reachable security issue in the supported release workflow.

title: Certificate signatures can verify with invalid signer keys
description: CertificateSignatureService now feeds all stored contact and own public certificates into the certificate-signature verifier and returns a .valid status plus resolved signer identity when the Rust verifier reports a cryptographic match. The Rust verifier's eligible_verification_keys function, however, enumerates the raw primary key and raw subkeys directly from the certificate and only checks whether a subkey has any self-signature containing the certification flag. It does not validate the candidate signer certificate with StandardPolicy, does not check revocation or expiration, and does not ensure the selected subkey is currently valid and bound to the primary under policy. As a result, a certification made by an expired/revoked signer key, or by a stale/untrusted certification-capable subkey that is no longer valid, can still be returned to the app as status .valid and attributed to the stored contact/own-key primary fingerprint. If UI or trust logic consumes this service as authoritative, an attacker with old compromised signing material or a crafted signer certificate can make attacker-controlled certifications appear to come from a trusted identity.
resolution_reason: Closing — by-design crypto-only verification (verified against current HEAD c26a071). The Rust verifier is intentionally crypto-only and documented as such (/// Crypto-only verification status, TDD §2.9 "workflow is crypto-only and does not mutate trust state automatically"). Verifying a signature against an expired/revoked key is cryptographically correct — the signature was legitimately produced before expiry/revocation; these are forward-looking validity concerns, not retroactive crypto invalidation. The attacker model is weak: forging a certification that verifies against a stored contact/own public key requires that key's private key; the "crafted signer certificate" path does not apply because candidate signers are the user's own stored contacts/keys, not attacker-controlled input. At HEAD the result is not consumed as authoritative trust — the contact-scoped UI shows a crypto "Status: Valid" plus resolved signer identity, gates only the saving of a cryptographic-evidence artifact, and per SECURITY.md does not mutate manual fingerprint verification or introduce web-of-trust. Note: the finding's primary mitigation ("no production caller") is outdated — the feature is now shipped and reachable, but the above keeps impact low/by-design. A possible future UX hardening (surfacing signer-key expiry/revocation alongside the crypto-valid result) is an enhancement, not a fix. Consistent with the finding's own matrix verdict (severity: ignore).

title: Signer selection is reset on every Sign view appearance
description: This commit replaced the previous one-time signer initialization with syncSignerFromDefaultOnAppear(), and SignView calls it on every onAppear. Because the method unconditionally assigns signerFingerprint from keyManagement.defaultKey, any user-selected non-default signer is lost when the Sign view appears again, for example after tab/navigation changes or returning from a system picker. The signing functions then use resolvedSignerFingerprint, so the produced signature may be made with the default key rather than the identity the user intentionally selected. This is an integrity/identity-confusion issue in signing workflows.
resolution_reason:
Closing as false positive — intentional, test-locked design (verified against current HEAD).
The described mechanism is accurate, but SignScreenModel.syncSignerFromDefaultOnAppear() re-syncing the signer to the current default key on every appearance is intentional and explicitly covered by tests — not a regression:
* SignScreenModelTests.test_syncSignerFromDefaultOnAppear_resetsManualSelectionToCurrentDefault asserts the exact "manual selection reverts to default on re-appear" behavior this finding flags.
* ..._recoversAfterDefaultKeyDeletion documents the rationale: re-syncing prevents signerFingerprint from holding a stale reference to a deleted/changed default key, which would otherwise break signing.
No security impact:
* The signing-key Picker is bound to the @Observable signerFingerprint, so the reset is visibly reflected in the UI before the user signs — there is no silent identity substitution. The resolved signer is always one of the user's own keys (never attacker-controlled), and signing is user-initiated and gated by Secure Enclave / biometric unwrap.
* The "returning from a system picker" trigger is inaccurate: file selection uses .fileImporter, a modal presented over SignView; dismissing it does not re-fire .onAppear. The reset only occurs on genuine re-appearance (tab/navigation), which is the intended sync point.
This is consistent with the finding's own attack-path matrix verdict (severity: ignore).

title: Unbounded SKESK S2K can exhaust memory
description: The commit adds password/SKESK decryption for attacker-supplied OpenPGP messages. During decrypt, the code collects every SKESK before the encrypted container, performs only symmetric/AEAD algorithm validation, and then calls `skesk.decrypt(password)` for each candidate. SKESK packets can carry expensive S2K parameters, including Argon2id in RFC 9580 messages. Unlike secret-key import, this path has no memory preflight, no maximum S2K cost, and no cap on the number of SKESK packets. A malicious password-encrypted message can therefore force very large Argon2id allocations or repeated expensive KDF work when the user attempts to decrypt it, causing an iOS Jetsam kill, crash, or prolonged hang. This affects availability rather than key confidentiality/integrity.
resolution_reason:
Close as will-not-fix / out of target threat model.

CA39 describes a potential resource-exhaustion issue in the password/SKESK service path: a maliciously constructed password-encrypted OpenPGP message could include costly or numerous SKESK/S2K candidates, causing repeated password derivation work before the message is rejected.

We agree this could at most affect local availability if the path were exposed to untrusted user input: the app may hang, consume CPU/memory, or terminate. It does not create a confidentiality or integrity failure, does not expose private keys or plaintext, and does not bypass AEAD/MDC authentication.

CypherAir’s current user-facing large-file and import paths already apply resource-conscious handling where those paths are part of the supported UI surface, such as streaming file operations and Argon2id memory checks for protected private-key import. The password/SKESK message feature, however, is currently service-only and is not part of the formally shipped UI surface. CypherAir does not plan to expose this password-message feature to users in the foreseeable future because the overall password-encryption product model does not meet our security expectations for a supported user-facing workflow.

For that reason, this issue is outside the current CypherAir threat model and will not be fixed at this time. If password/SKESK message support is ever promoted to a user-facing feature, this resource-budgeting concern should be re-evaluated before launch.

title: Legacy share temp files are no longer cleaned on launch
description: Before this commit, several ShareLink call sites materialized export data in FileManager.default.temporaryDirectory/share via Data.writeToShareTempFile. Those artifacts could include passphrase-protected private-key backups and revocation certificates. This commit stops creating new tmp/share files, which is an improvement, but it also removes the startup cleanup for that directory. Users upgrading from a prior version may already have tmp/share artifacts; after this change, the app no longer deletes them on launch, extending the lifetime of sensitive local files until the OS happens to purge the temporary directory. The fix should keep deleting tmp/share even if no current code writes there, at least as a migration cleanup.
resolution_reason: We won't fix this problem. It will not be used by the attackers. Old temporary files will be automatically cleaned up by the system. The new version of the software will no longer generate such temporary files, so there is no need to be equipped with cleaning.

title: AuthenticationManager target addition breaks builds
description: The Xcode project change makes AuthenticationManager.swift part of the CypherAir application target. That source file contains the @Observable macro, but only imports Foundation, LocalAuthentication, and Security. @Observable is provided by the Observation module and is not available through those imports, so compiling the target will fail with an unknown attribute error. This is a non-security build regression rather than an exploitable runtime vulnerability.
resolution_reason: False positive on current main. AuthenticationManager.swift is in the app target, but the current macOS build succeeds; the claimed @Observable / missing Observation import build failure is not reproducible on the latest main.

title: High Security uses biometryAny, enabling biometric reset bypass
description: High Security Mode is documented as biometric-only and explicitly intended to prevent access by an attacker who has both the device and the passcode. However, the newly added docs specify `SecAccessControlCreateFlags` of `[.privateKeyUsage, .biometryAny]` for High Security Mode. On Apple platforms, `.biometryAny` permits access by any currently enrolled biometric and does not invalidate the protected item when the enrolled biometric set changes. A passcode-compromising attacker may be able to enroll or reset biometrics and then satisfy the app's biometric-only prompt, bypassing the intended passcode-theft resistance. For this threat model, High Security Mode should use `.biometryCurrentSet` or equivalent enrollment-change invalidation/re-wrapping logic, while handling loss of access as an expected consequence.
resolution_reason: Closing as already fixed. The implementation intentionally uses .biometryAny for High Security, so this is not a code-level bypass against the documented current access-control policy. The issue was that PRD/SECURITY wording overstated the guarantee by implying protection against an attacker with both device and passcode in general. The docs have now been revised to state the precise guarantee: High Security denies device-passcode fallback for private-key operations, and SECURITY.md explicitly notes that .biometryAny does not invalidate keys merely because biometric enrollment changes.
